mnemonics.title: UX Process Steps
mnemonics.content: R-D-I-P-T-I: Research → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test → Iterate

mnemonics.title: Design Thinking Phases
mnemonics.content: EDIPT: Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test

mnemonics.title: User-Centered Design Principles
mnemonics.content: AIR H: Active user involvement, Iterative, Research-based, Holistic approach

mnemonics.title: UX Evaluation Metrics
mnemonics.content: SUS: Speed, Usability, Satisfaction - plus Task Completion

acronyms.usage: The overall experience a user has with a product
The visual and interactive elements of a product
The organization and structure of content in a product
Design approach focusing on user needs throughout development
Testing two variants to determine which performs better

visualTips.visualization: Think of wireframes as blueprints for a house - showing structure without colors, textures, or decorations. Simple rectangles and lines represent where elements will go.
Create mental picture of a real person representing your user type. Give them a name, photo, job, and goals to make design decisions more relatable.
Draw a four-quadrant box: What they SAY, What they THINK, What they DO, What they FEEL. Fill each with sticky notes during user research.
Visualize a library catalog system - organized shelves, clear signs, logical groupings helping users find what they need without frustration.
Think of a crowded desk vs. organized desk. Too much information overwhelms users; clean design is like a tidy workspace.

analogies.analogy: If a website were a restaurant: UI is the interior design, menu layout, and table settings (what you see), while UX is the entire dining experience from walking in to leaving satisfied (how you feel).
Like sculpting - you don't carve the final statue in one go. You chip away, step back, evaluate, and refine repeatedly until you achieve the desired form.
Like writing a character for a movie - you create a detailed backstory to understand and predict how that person would react in different situations.
Like an architectural floor plan - shows where walls, doors, and furniture go without paint colors or furniture styles.
A door handle affords pulling; a button affords pressing. Just as a physical object suggests its use, digital elements should suggest interactions.
